source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Mooring Line Failure at MC 941 Platform A

context.region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 941

actions_taken: Shut in production and reduced Personnel on Board to essential personnel only
Inspected remaining 3 mooring lines on Port Column
Attempted to increase tension on ML #9 by hauling in anchor chain on 20 June 2023
Conducted ROV inspection to locate H-link connector and anchor chain
Recovered anchor chain, H-link, and adjacent links from seabed on 2 July 2023
Sent failed components to Stress Engineering Services Inc. for metallurgical failure analysis
Conducted onsite BSEE investigation on 13 July 2023
event.summary: On 15 June 2023, during a scheduled underwater inspection of the Titan SPAR platform mooring system, abnormal slack was observed on Mooring Line #9 from the Port Column. Investigation revealed a significant length of the line was resting on the seabed. The 5-inch Grade RQ4 chain link subsequently failed due to fatigue initiating at the crown where stresses are high, with corrosion as a contributing factor. The failure was likely initiated by surface cracks on the crown. The incident resulted in production shutdown and a replacement cost estimate of $5,000,000.

hazards.name: High-stress mooring system under dynamic loading

threats.name: Fatigue crack initiation at chain link crown
Delayed detection of mooring system degradation

consequences.name: Loss of platform station keeping capability
Mooring line replacement and repair costs
Potential platform drift and collision risk

controls.name: In-Service Inspection Plan (ISIP) with American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) Underwater Inspection in lieu of Drydocking (UWILD)
controls.lod_basis: Scheduled inspection program to identify defects before failure

supporting_text: On 15 June 2023, in accordance with the In-Service Inspection Plan (ISIP), Titan was conducting a scheduled American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) Underwater Inspection in lieu of Drydocking (UWILD) when an abnormal amount of slack was observed on the ML #9

controls.name: Daily marine report monitoring of mooring line tension
controls.lod_basis: Routine monitoring to identify anomalies in real-time

supporting_text: on 11 March 2022, Titan operations found a significant decrease in ML #9 tension recorded on the daily marine report
on 9 September 2022, low tension and an associated alarm for ML #9 were noted during an internal technical integrity review

controls.name: Inspection of remaining mooring lines following detection of slack
controls.barrier_role: Verification of platform safety and station-keeping capability
controls.lod_basis: Rapid assessment of redundancy to maintain safe operations

supporting_text: The other 3 MLs on the Port Column were inspected and found to be in good condition
All 3 remaining MLs on the Port Column were found to be in good condition, rendering the platform safe to remain manned

controls.name: Production shutdown and personnel reduction following detection of mooring line failure
controls.barrier_role: Risk reduction during degraded mooring system state
controls.lod_basis: Operational response to reduce exposure during investigation and repair

supporting_text: Equinor shut in production and reduced the number of Personnel on Board (POB) to essential personnel only

controls.name: Maintenance management program for mooring system
controls.barrier_role: Preventive maintenance to avoid fatigue and corrosion failures
controls.lod_basis: Proactive maintenance to extend equipment life and prevent degradation

supporting_text: Management Systems – Weakness in maintenance management program for mooring system: Reliance on third party vendors to conduct corrective maintenance work orders on the mooring system caused delays due to unavailability

controls.name: ROV inspection and failure analysis of mooring line components
controls.barrier_role: Root cause determination and recovery planning
controls.lod_basis: Post-incident investigation to understand failure mechanism and prevent recurrence

supporting_text: Using a Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) to perform the inspection, the H-link connector was found buried at the seabed
The anchor chain, H-link, and adjacent links were recovered from the seabed and sent to Stress Engineering Services Inc. (SES) in Houston, TX for a detailed failure analysis

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
